html:
What a Full Reroof Actually Covers on a Springfield Commercial Building

A reroof quote in Springfield can mean full tear-off down to deck, or it can mean a recover system laid over whatever is already up there. Those are two different projects with two different price tags, two different warranties, and two different answers for how the building performs the next time a hailstorm rolls through southwest Missouri. We walk owners through what's actually being proposed before a number goes on paper.

Why "Reroof" Means Something Different on Every Bid

Ask five contractors for a reroof price on the same building and you'll often get five different scopes hiding behind the same word. One bid means a mechanically attached membrane over new insulation on a torn-off deck. Another means a fleece-back system glued down over the existing built-up roof with a thin cover board in between. Both get called "reroof" on the proposal. The owner comparing line totals has no way to know they're not comparing the same job.

That gap matters more in this market than most, because a lot of Springfield's commercial stock — the retail strips along Glenstone, the older flex buildings near the rail corridors, the distribution space that's gone up around the I-44 interchanges — has already been recovered once or twice. Every layer that stays on the deck adds weight, adds uncertainty about what's actually under the new membrane, and in some cases runs into code limits on how many roof layers a structure can carry.

We start every reroof conversation by naming which of those two projects we're actually pricing, then explain why.

Tear-Off vs. Recover: The Call That Sets Everything Else

Recover systems make sense when the existing roof is dry, the deck is sound, and there's no more than one layer already up there. They cost less and go faster. Tear-off is the right call when there's trapped moisture, a soft deck, ponding that a recover would just replicate, or when the building already carries the maximum layers code allows.

The only honest way to make that call is a core cut — a small sample pulled from the existing roof to see what's actually in the assembly and whether insulation underneath is wet. A quote written without one is a guess dressed up as a number. We cut cores before we price tear-off versus recover, and we show the owner what came out of the roof.

Deck condition changes the number too. Steel deck with surface rust prices differently than deck that's rotted through at a drain bowl. A quote that doesn't separate "expected deck repair" from "if we find something worse" is setting up a change order before the crew ever shows up.

What Southwest Missouri Weather Does Before a Leak Shows Up

Springfield sits in a stretch of the country that gets hit by hail more than most of the country realizes — spring and early summer storm cells moving through the Ozarks region routinely drop hail large enough to bruise a membrane without punching through it right away. That damage doesn't always show as a leak the same year. It shows up two or three years later as a membrane that's lost its granule coat or a seam that's started to fatigue faster than it should.

Winter works the roof from the other direction. An ice storm that glazes a low-slope roof and then thaws puts standing water exactly where drains are already marginal, and freeze-thaw cycling through a Missouri winter opens up flashing laps that were borderline to begin with. UV exposure through the summer does the slow work in between. A reroof scope that accounts for this climate specs a thicker membrane, more fastener density, or a different flashing detail at parapets than a roof going up in a milder region.

  • Tear-off and haul-away of the existing membrane, insulation, and any wet substrate found during demolition
  • Deck inspection with repair priced per square foot, not buried in a vague allowance
  • Insulation R-value specified by layer, with fastening or adhesive method named
  • Membrane type, thickness, and the actual manufacturer warranty term being sold
  • Flashing detail at every curb, penetration, and parapet — not "as required"
  • Drainage correction if the existing roof already shows signs of ponding

Reading a Reroof Quote Like a Roofer, Not a Buyer

Most of the reroof quotes we see next to our own share a few tells. "Flashing as needed" instead of a flashing detail. A tear-off allowance capped at a square footage that doesn't match the building's actual roof plan. A warranty listed by year count with no mention of whether it's a materials-only warranty or a full system warranty backed by the manufacturer's inspection of the installation.

None of that is illegal. It's just written so the number looks smaller on the page than the job will actually cost once the crew is on the roof and the change orders start. We itemize the same way we'd want to read a quote if we were the ones signing it — deck repair as its own line, tear-off scope tied to the actual roof plan, and the warranty spelled out by type, with more detail than a bare year count.

Timing a Reroof Around Storm Season and a Working Building

Most owners want reroof work scheduled outside the peak spring hail window when possible, and around occupied space when it isn't. A distribution building near the I-44 interchanges runs shipping on a schedule that doesn't pause for construction. A medical office building tied to the larger Springfield health systems has patients in the building during the workday. We sequence tear-off in sections so no more roof is open than can be dried in before weather, and we plan around loading docks and entrances that have to stay clear.

Reroof Questions We Hear on Walkthroughs

How do you decide between tear-off and recover?

We cut a core sample first. If the existing roof is dry, sound, and under the layer limit, recover is on the table. If there's trapped moisture or deck damage, tear-off is the only option that doesn't just seal a problem under a new membrane.

What happens if you find wet insulation once tear-off starts?

We stop, document it with photos, and price the additional scope before continuing rather than absorbing it into a change order after the fact.

How long does a reroof take on a typical Springfield building?

A mid-size flat roof usually runs one to three weeks depending on square footage, deck condition, and how the work gets sequenced around weather and building operations.

Will a recover system void anything on the deck below?

No, but it also won't fix anything wrong with what's underneath. That's why we only recommend recover once we've confirmed the existing roof is actually dry.

Do you reroof buildings that stay open during the work?

Yes. We section the roof, keep entrances and loading areas clear, and coordinate timing with whoever runs the building day to day.
